nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, clear to slightly turbid streams and tributaries of the upper Rio Guaporé and Rio Mamoré drainages; substrates of fine sand and leaf litter with slow to moderate current

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (essential for barbel health)
dense mid-background planting (Java fern, Vallisneria, Anubias)

diet.notes: Sinking pellets and wafers (Corydoras-specific or catfish pellets) form the dietary staple. Readily accepts frozen and live foods: bloodworm, white worm, tubifex, daphnia, and brine shrimp. Also scavenges uneaten flake food and organic detritus from the substrate. Feed after lights out for best results, as this species is most active in dim conditions.

goodWith: other Corydoras species of similar size
avoidWith: oscar and other large predatory fish
very small nano shrimp and fry (predation risk)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Corydoras haraldschultzi; verify availability before upload.

summary: A mid-sized Amazonian corydoras from Peru and Brazil, distinguished by an intricate dark reticulated mosaic pattern on a pale body. Peaceful, hardy, and easy to keep in groups of 6+; an ideal bottom-dweller for community aquariums with fine sand and soft to moderately hard water.
careNotes:
The Mosaic Corydoras (Corydoras haraldschultzi) is native to the upper Rio Guaporé and Rio Mamoré drainages of western Brazil and Peru, where it inhabits shallow, slow-moving streams over fine sandy substrates. It is frequently confused with C. sterbai, which shares overlapping range and similar patterning; C. haraldschultzi has a lighter base coloration and darker, more intricate reticulated markings, while C. sterbai tends to show a more orange-tinted fin coloration and dot-like spotting.

A fine sand substrate of at least 3 cm depth is essential — gravel can abrade the sensitive barbels and dermal skin, leading to infection. Sand allows the fish to sift naturally and keeps barbels intact. Provide shaded areas with driftwood, leaf litter, and dense planting; this species is most active in low light and benefits from a dusk-timed feeding schedule.

Keep in groups of 6 or more (ideally 10+) as C. haraldschultzi is a strongly social shoaling species; lone individuals or small groups exhibit stress-related behaviors, poor feeding response, and shortened lifespan. Weekly water changes of 25–30% maintain the low-nitrate environment this species prefers.

Note on taxonomy: a 2024 revision by Dias and Britto proposed reclassification of C. haraldschultzi to the genus Hoplisoma as Hoplisoma haraldschultzi; this reclassification is pending broader adoption in the hobby and trade literature.

Note on handling: like all Corydoras, C. haraldschultzi possesses stiffened, serrated pectoral and dorsal fin spines that can deliver a mildly venomous sting. Use caution when netting or handling and avoid net entanglement. The venom causes localized pain and swelling but is not medically serious in healthy adults.
breedingNotes: Corydoras haraldschultzi has been bred in captivity, though it is less commonly spawned than more readily available species such as C. paleatus or C. aeneus. Conditioning involves frequent small live and frozen food feeds (bloodworm, daphnia, brine shrimp) and a drop in water temperature of 3–5°C to simulate the onset of Amazonian wet season rains — a partial water change with slightly cooler, soft water is the standard trigger. Spawning is typical T-position for Corydoras: the female holds sperm in her mouth and pelvic fins while depositing 2–5 adhesive eggs on a cleaned flat surface (glass, broad plant leaf, or smooth rock). A typical spawning produces 100–200 eggs over several hours. Eggs should be removed to a separate, well-aerated hatching container and treated with methylene blue to prevent fungal infection. Fry hatch in 4–6 days at 24°C and become free-swimming 2–3 days later. Feed with infusoria or commercial fry food initially, transitioning to microworms and baby brine shrimp within the first week.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras haraldschultzi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Rio Guaporé system, Brazil/Bolivia border), water parameters (temperature 20–26°C, pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 36–357 ppm), adult size (males to ~5.4 cm, females slightly larger), diet, behaviour, and reproduction notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Corydoras haraldschultzi
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes), adult size (to 6.5 cm TL), native range (Rio Guaporé and Rio Mamoré, Brazil and Peru), and temperature range (22–26°C). Confirmed venomous spine status consistent with Corydoras genus.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Corydoras Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General Corydoras care resource; confirmed minimum group size of 6, fine sand substrate requirement, omnivore diet, community compatibility, and easy care level classification. Supplemented species-specific data from SeriouslyFish and FishBase.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Corydoras identification and care
additional.notes: Used for confirmation of C. haraldschultzi vs C. sterbai identification notes, mosaic pattern description, and western Amazon provenance. Also referenced hobbyist confusion between the two species.

additional.name: Dias & Britto (2024) — Hoplisoma reclassification reference
additional.notes: Reference for the 2024 Dias/Britto taxonomic revision proposing reclassification of Corydoras haraldschultzi to Hoplisoma haraldschultzi. Pending broad adoption in hobby and trade literature at time of data entry.

fish.breedingNotes: Bred in captivity via cool-water trigger (temperature drop 3–5°C) and conditioning with live/frozen foods. T-position spawning on flat surfaces; 100–200 adhesive eggs per spawn. Fry are straightforward to raise on infusoria and baby brine shrimp.
